sanskrit_devanagari:
अनन्यचेताः सततं यो मां स्मरति नित्यशः ।
तस्याहं सुलभः पार्थ नित्ययुक्तस्य योगिनः ॥ १४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
ananya-cetāḥ satataṃ yo māṃ smarati nityaśaḥ |
tasyāhaṃ sulabhaḥ pārtha nitya-yuktasya yoginaḥ || 14 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; vedabase renders verse as concatenated text but body matches the canonical reading word-for-word).
exact (Mukundananda house style: h-aspirate marks on śh, single danda visarga; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_08.014 = MBh_06,030.014). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: with mind not turned to anything else

translations.verbatim_quote: For one who always remembers Me without deviation, I am easy to obtain, O son of Pṛthā, because of his constant engagement in devotional service.
BG 8.14: O Parth, for those yogis who always think of Me with exclusive devotion, I am easily attainable because of their constant absorption in Me.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: This verse especially describes the final destination attained by the unalloyed devotees who serve the Supreme Personality of Godhead in bhakti-yoga. Previous verses have mentioned four different kinds of devotees – the distressed, the inquisitive, those who seek material gain, and the speculative philosophers.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use, ≤300 chars of full bhāṣya per IP rules)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.14 Partha, O son of Prtha, tasya yoginah, to that yogi; nitya-yuktasya, of constant concentration, who is ever absorbed (in God); and ananya-cetah, of single-minded attention, a yogi whose mind is not drawn to any other object; yah, who; smarati, remembers; mam, Me, the supreme God; satatam, constantly.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.14 I am easy to access to that Yogin who is ‘ever integrated with Me,’ i.e., who wants constant contact with Me, who recollects Me; and whose mind is not in ‘anything else without break’ (Nityasah), i.e., at the time of meditation and also during all other times (Satatam).

positions.rendering: of single-minded attention; one whose mind is not drawn to any other object — single-pointed concentration on God
of mind not in anything else; the love-of-God exclusivity that is bhakti's interior signature
without deviation of the mind; the deviation-free remembrance characteristic of devotional service

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Traditions agree on the structural claim (constancy + single-pointedness produces ease of attainment). They differ on the affect: Shankara reads ananya-cetāḥ as a cognitive single-pointedness; Ramanuja and Prabhupada read it as the affective exclusivity of devotional love. The verse itself supports both readings.

literal_meaning: Whoever, with mind turned to nothing else, constantly remembers Me always — to him I am easily attainable, O son of Pṛthā, to that constantly-yoked yogi.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names the form of practice (constant, single-pointed remembrance — ananya-cetāḥ smarati nityaśaḥ) and its consequence: ease of attainment (sulabhaḥ). The structural force is the contrast between situational practice and constant practice. The yogi who remembers daily, not occasionally, is called nitya-yuktaḥ (ever-yoked); to such a one, Krishna's attainment is easy. Shankara emphasizes nitya-yuktasya as 'of constant concentration, ever absorbed in God'; ananya-cetāḥ as 'mind not drawn to any other object.' Ramanuja reads it as the constant contact with Krishna at meditation-time and at all other times (satatam). Prabhupada and Mukundananda read it within the bhakti frame as the constancy of devotional remembrance.
